---
title: llms.txt
llms-description: Making your docs LLM-friendly with auto-generated llms.txt and per-page Markdown sources.
---

## Index Page

Inspect Docs produces an [`llms.txt`](https://llmstxt.org) index page at the root of your site and a plain-Markdown source for every rendered page, giving language models a structured entry point and clean raw markdown content to work with.

The file is built from your site's `title`, `description`, `navigation`, and reference pages, and looks roughly like this:

```markdown
# My Package

> Description of what the package does.

## Docs

- [Getting Started](https://docs.example.com/index.html): Install the package and render your first site.
- [Guides](https://docs.example.com/guides/index.html): Task-oriented walkthroughs.

## API Reference

- [my_package](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package.html): Core tasks and utilities.
- [my_package.utils](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package.utils.html): Helper functions.

## CLI Reference

- [my_package run](https://docs.example.com/reference/my_package_run.html): Run a task.
```

### Descriptions

Each entry in `llms.txt` uses the page's frontmatter `description` field as its summary. Writing a short `description` on every article and reference page is the single most impactful thing you can do to make `llms.txt` useful — both for LLMs and for the auto-generated reference index that uses the same field.

Sometimes the summary you want to show *on* a page is different from the summary you want to expose to LLMs. The page description might be marketing-flavored ("Get up and running in five minutes!") while the LLM description should be more literal ("Installation, first render, and project layout").

Set `llms-description` in the page frontmatter to override just the `llms.txt` entry:

```yaml
---
title: Getting Started
description: Get up and running in five minutes!
llms-description: Installation, first render, and project layout.
---
```

If `llms-description` is absent, `description` is used. If both are absent, the page appears in `llms.txt` with an empty summary.

## Markdown Pages

In addition to the HTML version of each page, Inspect Docs publishes a plain-Markdown version at the same URL with `.html.md` appended. 

So a page rendered at `https://docs.example.com/guides/install.html` is also available at `https://docs.example.com/guides/install.html.md` as raw Markdown.

Inspect Docs also injects a **Copy page** button into every page header. The button lets readers:

- **Copy as Markdown** — copy the page's Markdown source to the clipboard.
- **Open as Markdown** — open the `.html.md` version in a new tab.
